Motion For Judgment On Part Of The Pleadings, As To Causes Of Action Numbers One And Two In Plaintiffs Third Amended Complaint, Without Leave To Amend.
Off Calendar Feb-28-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA COLLEGE OF THE ARTS' Motion For Judgment On Part Of The Pleadings, As To Causes Of Action Numbers One And Two In Plaintiffs Third Amended Complaint, Without Leave To Amend.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion for judgment on the pleadings (MJP) as to counts 1 and 2 of the third amended complaint (TAC) is granted for lack of standing.

The same standing issues were already thoroughly addressed in the MJP proceedings on the second amended complaint (SAC), and the September 14, 2023 order granting that motion is thus incorporated by reference. In sum, the September order found that Corporations Code 5142 sets the universe of individuals who have standing to file an action for breach of charitable trust against a nonprofit public benefit corporation, and a plaintiff must have standing at the time of filing her complaint. Plaintiff lacks standing, inter alia, because she sued when no longer employed by defendant. (9/14/23 Order.)

After filing of the TAC, the second MJP and plaintiff's opposition, this court noted that the standing issues "appear largely identical" to those already addressed on the first MJP. (1/16/24 Order 1:3-6.) Hence, plaintiff was authorized to file a supplemental brief identifying "how the TAC's allegations differ from the SAC's allegations in relevant regards and why plaintiff believes those differences should yield different results than the court's September 14, 2023 order." (1/16/24 Order 1:7-11.)

The supplemental brief makes two arguments, both unavailing. (Id. at 1:2-17.)
First, plaintiff says the TAC differs from the SAC in alleging attempts to "give notice to the Board Corporations Code [sic] while she was employed" by defendant. (Supp. Brf. 1:3-7 [emphasis in original].) But the SAC made many allegations about notice plaintiff gave while employed, and they were thoroughly addressed in the first MJP proceedings. (See, e.g., SAC 3:13-14, 4:23-25, 8:12, 11:10-16, 29:7-30:18, 42:17-22, 44:1, 45:18-24, 47:4-8, 57:5-27; 7/3/23 Opp. 3:9-14; 9/14/23 Order.)

Second, plaintiff says section 5142 standing is not needed, because she has "special interest" standing. (Supp. Brf. 1:8-17.) Again, plaintiff made the same argument in the MJP proceedings on the SAC - and it too was thoroughly addressed. (See, e.g., 7/3/23 Opp. 1:19-26, 5:15-8:9; 9/14/23 Order.)

The motion is granted with prejudice because plaintiff - who has been admitted to the California bar - had four opportunities to plead cognizable claims.

Off Calendar Sep-14-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
Ross Vartain, Esq., VARTAIN LAW GROUP, P.C., ross@vartainlaw.com, for Defendant CALIFORNIA COLLEGE OF THE ARTS
No Apeparance is made by Plaintiff's counsel.

Hearing is held. Discussed, not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA COLLEGE OF THE ARTS Motion For Judgment Of The Pleadings As To Causes Of Action Numbers One Through Seven In Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint [C.C.P. 438, Et Seq.]

Defendant California College of the Arts' (CCA) "motion for judgment on the pleadings as to causes of action number one through seven in plaintiff's second amended complaint" (SAC) is granted with leave to amend.

CCA hired pro per plaintiff June Beltran in 2017. Part of her job was to direct CCA's Center for Art & Public Life, later renamed the Center for Impact (the Center). Beltran was fired in November 2019 and she sued in June 2021. The first seven of the SAC's 21 counts allege that CCA misused charitable donations designated for the Center and for other purposes; the other 14 counts are employment-related. CCA's motion asserts that Beltran lacked standing to bring the first seven claims.

CCA argues that Corporations Code 5142 "defines the complete universe of individuals who have standing to file an action for breach of charitable trust against a nonprofit public benefit corporation" and the plaintiff must have "standing at the time of filing the complaint in order to sue." Beltran disputes the former point with common law arguments, but her 24 pages of briefing do not appear to grapple with the latter point - and it is dispositive. Beltran was some 18 months removed from her CCA post when she sued and thus lacked standing at that time. The recent caselaw regards whether a plaintiff must maintain her basis for standing after she sues, thus assuming that standing is required at the time she sues. (See, e.g., Turner v. Victoria (Cal. Aug. 3, 2023, No. S271054); Summers v. Colette (2019) 67 Cal.App.5th 361.)

Beltran also does not dispute CCA's point that all seven of her counts are for breach of charitable trust no matter how they are labeled.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
